moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, claiming central space and freeing both our bishop and queen for development. This move controls the important d5 and f5 squares, giving us excellent central influence from the very first move. The e4 advance is one of the most principled openings because it immediately fights for the center while preparing rapid piece development. We're expecting Black to respond with either 1...e5 (leading to open games) or 1...c5 (the Sicilian), but we need to be ready for more unusual defenses that attack our pawn immediately.

Black chooses the provocative Alekhine Defense, immediately attacking our e4 pawn with the knight and inviting us to chase it around the board with our pawns. This hypermodern opening philosophy says that Black will allow us to build a big pawn center, then try to undermine and attack it later with pieces. The knight on f6 also prepares to retreat to squares like d5, e8, or h5 depending on how we continue. While this defense can lead to sharp tactical games, it's also quite risky for Black since the knight will be repeatedly attacked and may struggle to find a good home.

We're developing our bishop to an aggressive square that immediately targets Black's vulnerable f7 point, the weakest square in Black's position since it's only defended by the king. This move is the hallmark of the Krejcik Variation, choosing rapid development and attacking chances over the more common pawn advances like 2.e5. The bishop on c4 also supports a potential d3-d4 pawn advance later and keeps maximum pressure on Black's kingside. By developing rather than pushing pawns, we're betting that Black's knight will become a target that we can exploit with our active pieces.

Black boldly captures our e4 pawn, accepting the challenge and grabbing material while the knight finds a strong central outpost on e4. This is the critical moment of the Krejcik Gambit - Black is essentially calling our bluff and saying 'prove that your attacking chances are worth a whole pawn.' The knight on e4 is temporarily well-placed, controlling important squares like d2, f2, c5, and d6, but it may become vulnerable to our pieces. Black is counting on being able to consolidate the extra pawn while keeping the knight actively placed, but this capture opens up tactical possibilities for us.

We're playing the point of the entire gambit - sacrificing our bishop with check to expose Black's king and create immediate tactical complications! This forcing move gives check, so Black must respond to the king's safety first, and after the king moves (likely to e7 or f8), we'll have disrupted Black's kingside completely. The bishop sacrifice opens up the e-file for potential future attacks and makes castling very difficult for Black. While we're now down both a pawn and a bishop in pure material terms, Black's king safety problems and our lead in development should give us excellent practical compensation - this is exactly the kind of sharp, tactical position the Krejcik Gambit aims to create.
